item.title: EMBL ARISE2 Fellowships 2026 in Europe | Fully Funded Research Infrastructure Fellowship
item.dc:creator: Evelyn Essuman
item.description:
Applications are now open for the EMBL ARISE2 Fellowships, an exciting fully funded postdoctoral programme offered by the European Molecular Biology Laboratory (EMBL). Designed for outstanding early-career researchers, ARISE2 combines cutting-edge research with professional development, enabling fellows to build successful careers in research infrastructures across Europe. Unlike traditional postdoctoral fellowships, ARISE2 focuses on preparing scientists […]

The post EMBL ARISE2 Fellowships 2026 in Europe | Fully Funded Research Infrastructure Fellowship appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
Applications are now open for the EMBL ARISE2 Fellowships, an exciting fully funded postdoctoral programme offered by the European Molecular Biology Laboratory (EMBL). Designed for outstanding early-career researchers, ARISE2 combines cutting-edge research with professional development, enabling fellows to build successful careers in research infrastructures across Europe.

Unlike traditional postdoctoral fellowships, ARISE2 focuses on preparing scientists for leadership roles in research infrastructures. Fellows work on innovative technology or method development projects while receiving specialized training in project management, innovation, service delivery, entrepreneurship, and scientific leadership.

If you are passionate about advancing scientific technologies while developing the skills needed to support world-class research facilities, the EMBL ARISE2 Fellowship offers an exceptional career opportunity.

About the EMBL ARISE2 Fellowships

The Career Accelerator for Research Infrastructure Scientists (ARISE2) is the successor to the highly successful ARISE programme at EMBL.

The programme recruits talented postdoctoral researchers to work for three years at one of EMBL’s research sites. During the fellowship, participants undertake technology or method development projects within EMBL research groups and core facilities while participating in a structured professional development programme.

ARISE2 aims to train the next generation of research infrastructure scientists by combining scientific excellence with practical leadership skills, ensuring fellows are well-equipped to contribute to international research infrastructures and scientific innovation.

Fellowship Benefits

Successful fellows will receive:

  • Fully funded three-year postdoctoral appointment
  • Competitive salary and employee benefits
  • Hands-on experience at one of EMBL’s research sites
  • Access to world-class research infrastructure and facilities
  • Professional training in:
    • Leadership
    • Project management
    • Technology development
    • Innovation
    • Entrepreneurship
    • Service delivery
  • International networking opportunities
  • Career development support
  • Collaboration with leading scientists and technology experts across Europe

Research Areas

Fellows will contribute to technology and method development projects in research infrastructures across various life science disciplines.

Projects may involve:

  • Advanced imaging technologies
  • Bioinformatics
  • Structural biology
  • Genomics
  • Molecular biology
  • Data science
  • Artificial intelligence applications in biology
  • Microscopy
  • Proteomics
  • Scientific instrumentation
  • Computational biology

Eligibility Criteria

Applicants should:

  • Hold a PhD (or have completed all requirements before starting the fellowship).
  • Demonstrate strong research achievements.
  • Be interested in technology or method development.
  • Have excellent communication and teamwork skills.
  • Be motivated to pursue careers in research infrastructures.
  • Meet the mobility and eligibility requirements outlined by EMBL.

Researchers from around the world are encouraged to apply, provided they satisfy the programme’s eligibility requirements.

Programme Structure

ARISE2 Fellows are employed for three years at one of EMBL’s research sites.

Each fellow will:

  • Join an EMBL research group or core facility.
  • Conduct a technology or method development project.
  • Receive advanced career development training.
  • Participate in innovation and entrepreneurship activities.
  • Learn project and service management.
  • Develop leadership skills for research infrastructures.

The programme combines scientific research with professional skills that prepare fellows for leadership positions beyond traditional academic careers.

Why Apply?

The EMBL ARISE2 Fellowship offers much more than research funding.

Participants gain:

  • International research experience
  • Practical technology development expertise
  • Leadership training
  • Innovation and entrepreneurship skills
  • Exposure to Europe’s leading life science research infrastructure
  • Long-term career development support

The fellowship is particularly valuable for researchers interested in scientific services, advanced technologies, and research infrastructure management.

How to Apply

Interested applicants should carefully review the programme requirements before preparing their applications.

Applications must be submitted through the official EMBL application system before the published deadline.

Applicants are encouraged to prepare all required documents well in advance and ensure they meet the eligibility criteria before submission.

Official Programme Page:
https://www.embl.org/about/info/arise/

Application Portal:
https://www.embl.org/about/info/arise/apply/

Why Choose EMBL?

EMBL is one of the world’s leading research organizations in molecular biology and life sciences. With multiple research sites across Europe, EMBL provides scientists with access to cutting-edge facilities, interdisciplinary collaboration, and an international research environment.

Through ARISE2, fellows become part of a vibrant scientific community dedicated to advancing research technologies while developing the next generation of research infrastructure leaders.

Conclusion

The EMBL ARISE2 Fellowships provide an outstanding opportunity for postdoctoral researchers seeking to combine scientific excellence with leadership development in research infrastructures. By offering a fully funded position, world-class mentoring, and professional training, the programme prepares fellows for impactful careers at the forefront of scientific innovation.

Researchers interested in technology development, interdisciplinary collaboration, and research infrastructure are encouraged to apply and take advantage of this unique opportunity at EMBL.

Discover more fellowship opportunities like this HERE

The post EMBL ARISE2 Fellowships 2026 in Europe | Fully Funded Research Infrastructure Fellowship appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.

item.title: Blue Carbon Hub Early Career Ocean Professionals Program 2026: Fully Funded Eight-Week Placement in Perth, Australia
item.dc:creator: Muhammad Chishti
item.description:
Applications Open for the Blue Carbon Hub Early Career Ocean Professionals Program 2026 The Blue Carbon Hub is accepting applications for the 2026 Early Career Ocean Professionals Program, an intensive international placement designed to strengthen the skills, knowledge and professional networks of emerging ocean professionals from Indian Ocean Rim Association Member States. Successful participants will […]

The post Blue Carbon Hub Early Career Ocean Professionals Program 2026: Fully Funded Eight-Week Placement in Perth, Australia appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
Applications Open for the Blue Carbon Hub Early Career Ocean Professionals Program 2026

The Blue Carbon Hub is accepting applications for the 2026 Early Career Ocean Professionals Program, an intensive international placement designed to strengthen the skills, knowledge and professional networks of emerging ocean professionals from Indian Ocean Rim Association Member States.

Successful participants will travel to Perth, Western Australia, to undertake a focused project with the Blue Carbon Hub at the Indian Ocean Marine Research Centre. The program will run from 5 October to 27 November 2026, providing approximately eight weeks for participants to develop and complete projects connected to blue carbon science, finance or policy.

The initiative is intended to produce practical knowledge and recommendations that can help Indian Ocean Rim Association Member States protect, restore and sustainably manage blue carbon ecosystems. Participants will be expected to work closely with the Blue Carbon Hub and, where applicable, a nominated project collaborator throughout the placement.

Why Blue Carbon Ecosystems Matter

Blue carbon ecosystems are coastal and marine environments that capture, store and manage significant quantities of carbon. They are closely connected with environmental protection, economic security, climate resilience and the wellbeing of coastal communities.

According to the Blue Carbon Hub’s official call, these ecosystems contribute to:

  • Food security;
  • Sustainable livelihoods;
  • Climate change mitigation;
  • Climate change adaptation;
  • Reduced disaster risk;
  • Coastal resilience;
  • Protection from coastal hazards; and
  • Long-term environmental sustainability.

When blue carbon ecosystems are degraded, they may shift from functioning as carbon sinks to becoming major sources of greenhouse gas emissions. Their deterioration can therefore intensify climate risks while weakening the natural and economic systems upon which coastal communities depend.

Strengthening scientific knowledge, developing effective public policies, creating appropriate governance frameworks and identifying sustainable financial pathways are therefore essential for their protection and restoration. The Early Career Ocean Professionals Program responds to these needs by bringing emerging professionals into a practical environment where they can contribute research, analysis and policy-oriented solutions.

Program Location and Duration

The 2026 program will be hosted by the Blue Carbon Hub at the Indian Ocean Marine Research Centre in Perth, Australia.

Important program dates

  • Application deadline: Midnight on 7 August 2026
  • Notification of application outcome: By 31 August 2026
  • Program starting date: 5 October 2026
  • Program ending date: 27 November 2026
  • Expected commitment: Eight weeks in Australia

The online application form specifically asks applicants whether they can commit to eight weeks in Australia. It provides response options for applicants who can attend the full period, cannot attend or are available for six weeks or less. Applicants should therefore assess their availability carefully before submitting an application.

What Participants Will Do During the Program

Selected early-career professionals will undertake a focused, self-directed project with the Blue Carbon Hub. The project should fall within one of the program’s three main areas:

  1. Blue carbon science
  2. Blue carbon finance
  3. Blue carbon policy

Applicants are encouraged to propose their own innovative project ideas rather than selecting from a predetermined list of assignments. Projects may involve original analysis, synthesis of existing knowledge, preparation of policy recommendations or other practical outputs relevant to blue carbon protection and restoration.

The application form requires applicants to identify whether their proposed project falls under science, finance or policy. Applicants must also provide a project title, a detailed project description and an explanation of how the proposed work will be broadly relevant to IORA Member States.

Types of Projects That May Be Proposed

The program allows a variety of project formats, provided that the proposed activity is relevant to the program’s blue carbon themes and can realistically be completed during the placement.

Projects may include:

  • Original scientific research;
  • Reviews of academic or technical literature;
  • Policy briefs;
  • Finance-related analysis;
  • Governance or institutional studies;
  • Evaluation of blue carbon conservation strategies;
  • Development of practical recommendations;
  • Comparative analysis across IORA Member States;
  • Limited field-based activities;
  • Data analysis;
  • Preparation of regional knowledge products; or
  • Other innovative activities connected with blue carbon science, finance or policy.

The official guidance emphasizes that projects should be focused, achievable and designed to produce meaningful outcomes within the eight-week placement period. Participants are expected to complete their projects while working with the Blue Carbon Hub.

Potential Blue Carbon Project Areas

Although the official call does not prescribe a fixed list of project topics, applicants may consider developing proposals related to areas such as:

  • Conservation and restoration of mangroves;
  • Seagrass ecosystem protection;
  • Coastal wetland management;
  • Blue carbon measurement and accounting;
  • Climate finance for coastal restoration;
  • Community-based conservation models;
  • Governance of blue carbon ecosystems;
  • Coastal resilience and disaster-risk reduction;
  • Sustainable livelihoods connected with coastal resources;
  • Policy frameworks for ecosystem restoration;
  • Regional blue carbon collaboration;
  • Financing mechanisms for conservation projects;
  • Integration of blue carbon into national climate commitments;
  • Evaluation of restoration initiatives; and
  • Institutional capacity for blue carbon policy implementation.

These examples are not an official list of approved subjects. Applicants who are uncertain whether their idea falls within the program’s scope are encouraged to send a project summary to the designated Blue Carbon Hub contact before submitting the application.

Project Collaborator Requirements

Applicants are encouraged to nominate a person with whom they would like to collaborate during the placement. The nominated collaborator should be available for regular engagement throughout the program and should have relevant knowledge or expertise that can support the proposed project.

The collaborator may be based anywhere in the world. However, collaborators from IORA Member States are strongly preferred because one of the program’s objectives is to strengthen regional professional networks, institutional knowledge and technical capacity.

The official FAQ indicates that collaborators based at the Indian Ocean Marine Research Centre may be particularly useful because participants will be undertaking most of their activities at the Blue Carbon Hub’s Perth offices.

Applicants who do not already have an appropriate professional network may request assistance in identifying a suitable collaborator. To seek this assistance, applicants are encouraged to send a one-paragraph summary of their proposed project to Mat Vanderklift before submitting the full application.

The application form requests the name of the proposed collaborator, the collaborator’s role and their institutional affiliation. Applicants should therefore contact potential collaborators in advance and ensure that the proposed individual understands the project and can engage regularly during the program.

Financial Support and Expenses Covered

The Blue Carbon Hub Early Career Ocean Professionals Program provides substantial financial support for selected participants.

The official call states that the program will cover major visit-related expenses, including:

  • International airfares;
  • Accommodation during the placement;
  • Eligible Australian visa application fees; and
  • Per diem support for participants.

The FAQ explains that participants will receive a weekly per diem intended to cover meals, local transportation and other out-of-pocket expenses during the program.

Expenses covered by the program

Selected participants can expect support for:

  1. Air travel

Major airfare costs associated with travelling to Australia for the placement are covered under the program.

  1. Accommodation

Accommodation will be arranged for participants during the program period.

  1. Visa application expenses

Eligible visa application costs will be reimbursed by the Blue Carbon Hub.

  1. Meals and daily living expenses

Participants will receive per diem support to help cover meals, transport and other routine out-of-pocket expenses during the placement.

What is not covered

The official call clearly states that the program does not provide a salary. The opportunity should therefore be understood as a funded professional placement rather than salaried employment.

The published documents also do not promise unlimited funding for fieldwork, extensive laboratory activities or major research-related expenses. Applicants proposing such activities must clearly disclose them and provide estimated costs.

Accommodation Arrangements in Perth

The Blue Carbon Hub will arrange accommodation for participants and will make reasonable efforts to secure housing close to its office in Perth. However, accommodation near the office cannot be guaranteed.

Successful participants will generally share accommodation with other members of the program. The housing arrangement may include both male and female participants.

Each participant will normally have a private bedroom, while facilities such as the following will be shared:

  • Kitchen;
  • Bathroom;
  • Living area; and
  • Other common spaces.

The Blue Carbon Hub states that it cannot provide separate or individual accommodation. The application form therefore asks applicants to confirm whether they are comfortable residing in a shared household that may include people of another gender.

Applicants should consider this arrangement carefully before applying because accepting shared accommodation appears to be an important practical condition of participation.

Australian Visa Process

Successful candidates will be responsible for applying for the appropriate Australian visa. The Blue Carbon Hub will not submit the visa application on behalf of the participant, although eligible visa application costs will be reimbursed.

Selected applicants should begin the visa process promptly after receiving confirmation because the outcome notification is expected by 31 August 2026, while the program begins on 5 October 2026. This creates a relatively limited period for completing visa, travel and administrative arrangements.

Applicants should ensure that their passport and supporting documents are current and readily available. Final visa issuance will remain subject to Australian immigration requirements and decisions.

Fieldwork and In Situ Data Collection

Projects may include fieldwork or in situ data collection when such activities are appropriate to the project. However, funding for field-based activities is limited, and most work is expected to be completed at the Blue Carbon Hub offices in Perth.

Applicants proposing fieldwork must clearly explain:

  • Why the fieldwork is necessary;
  • Where it will take place;
  • What activities will be conducted;
  • How it contributes to the project;
  • What resources will be required; and
  • The estimated costs involved.

Modest fieldwork-related expenses may be considered, depending on the availability of funding. Applicants should not assume that extensive travel, large-scale field surveys or expensive data-collection activities will be financed.

The online application form asks applicants to indicate whether their project includes fieldwork in Australia and to explain anticipated costs where fieldwork may be required.

Laboratory Space and Equipment

The application form also asks whether the proposed project will require access to laboratory space or equipment while the participant is in Australia.

Applicants can indicate:

  • Yes;
  • No; or
  • Maybe.

Applicants who select yes or maybe are asked to provide additional information and explain their expected requirements and costs.

Applicants should therefore investigate the likely technical requirements of their projects before applying. A proposal that requires specialized laboratory facilities should clearly explain why those facilities are essential and how they will contribute to an achievable eight-week outcome.

Sending Samples to Australia

The program may permit participants to send samples to Australia for analysis. However, applicants must disclose this intention in their applications.

Successful applicants proposing to send samples will receive further guidance on:

  • Types of samples permitted;
  • Quantities required;
  • Packaging;
  • Documentation;
  • Australian customs requirements; and
  • Other administrative procedures.

Because customs processes may cause delays, applicants considering sample-based work are strongly encouraged to begin planning early. The application form specifically asks candidates whether they intend to send samples to Australia as part of their projects.

Eligibility Criteria

Applicants must meet the program’s citizenship or residency and academic-timing requirements.

Citizenship or residency requirement

Applications are open to people who are either:

  • Citizens of an IORA Member State; or
  • Residents of an IORA Member State.

Applicants who are neither citizens nor residents of an IORA Member State are not eligible. Successful applicants may be required to submit documentation confirming their citizenship or legal residency status.

Early-career academic requirement

Applicants must be within six years of graduating from their highest degree.

The official FAQ identifies the relevant highest-degree categories as:

  • Bachelor’s degree;
  • Master’s degree; or
  • Doctoral degree.

The online form also contains an Honours option when applicants identify their highest qualification.

Applicants should calculate the six-year period from the date on which they completed their highest qualification. The official materials do not publish an age limit; eligibility is based on the period since completion of the applicant’s highest degree rather than the applicant’s age.

Current students

The online application form asks applicants whether they are currently studying and, where applicable, when they expect to complete their studies. However, the published FAQ defines eligibility as being within six years of graduating from the applicant’s highest degree. Current students should seek clarification from the Blue Carbon Hub where their eligibility is uncertain.

Institutional affiliation

Applicants must submit a letter of support from their host institution. This means candidates should have an institution willing to formally support their participation and proposed project.

Application Documents Required

The official FAQ identifies three principal application documents:

  1. Current curriculum vitae

The CV should present the applicant’s education, professional experience, research background, technical skills, publications, projects, policy work and other relevant achievements.

  1. Letter of support from the host institution

Applicants must obtain a formal institutional support letter. They should request this document early enough to avoid missing the deadline.

  1. Outline of the proposed project

The proposal should clearly explain what the applicant intends to undertake during the placement and how it relates to blue carbon science, finance or policy.

The online form allows applicants to upload the CV and institutional support letter. The form states a maximum file size of 100 MB for each upload.

Information Requested in the Online Application Form

Applicants should be prepared to provide the following information:

  • Full name;
  • Email address;
  • Telephone number;
  • Organisation or institution;
  • Country represented;
  • Highest degree;
  • Graduation date;
  • Current study status;
  • Expected date of study completion, where applicable;
  • Proposed project category;
  • Project title;
  • Detailed project description;
  • Explanation of the project’s relevance to IORA Member States;
  • Proposed collaborator’s name;
  • Collaborator’s role and institutional affiliation;
  • Confirmation of availability for eight weeks;
  • Information about samples being sent to Australia;
  • Information about proposed fieldwork;
  • Information about required laboratory facilities;
  • Anticipated fieldwork or laboratory costs;
  • Acceptance of the shared accommodation arrangement;
  • Current CV; and
  • Letter of support from the applicant’s host institution.

Applicants should prepare these details before opening the form to ensure that their responses are consistent, complete and supported by the uploaded documents.

Relevance to IORA Member States

A central requirement of the application is explaining how the proposed project will be broadly relevant to IORA Member States.

Applicants should avoid presenting a project as an isolated study with no regional implications. A strong project outline should explain how the expected findings, recommendations, tools or lessons could support blue carbon protection and restoration in one or more Indian Ocean countries.

Regional relevance could be demonstrated through:

  • Shared coastal-management challenges;
  • Replicable restoration approaches;
  • Regional policy recommendations;
  • Cross-country learning;
  • Common financing barriers;
  • Comparable governance arrangements;
  • Climate adaptation priorities;
  • Community resilience considerations;
  • Capacity-building opportunities; or
  • Potential collaboration among IORA institutions.

The application form directly asks applicants to explain the broader relevance of their project to IORA Member States.

IORA National Focal Point Endorsement

Final participation is subject to endorsement by the relevant IORA National Focal Point. The call brief states that this endorsement will be coordinated through the IORA Secretariat.

Applicants should not confuse endorsement with ordinary institutional support. The application requires a support letter from the applicant’s host institution, while final participation also requires endorsement through the relevant IORA mechanism.

For questions about the endorsement process, applicants may contact the IORA Secretariat representative identified in the official FAQ.

Selection Process

Successful participants will be selected through a competitive assessment process.

The reviewed call brief and FAQ do not publish a detailed scoring rubric. Applicants should therefore not assume that any unofficial selection criteria are guaranteed. However, the application requirements indicate that candidates should pay particular attention to:

  • Their compliance with citizenship or residency requirements;
  • Their early-career eligibility;
  • The clarity and feasibility of the proposed project;
  • The relevance of the project to IORA Member States;
  • The project’s connection with science, finance or policy;
  • The ability to complete the project within eight weeks;
  • The availability of an appropriate collaborator;
  • The clarity of fieldwork or laboratory requirements;
  • Institutional support; and
  • Availability for the full placement period.

These points are practical preparation considerations derived from the application questions and official program requirements rather than a formally published scoring framework.

How to Develop a Competitive Project Proposal

Applicants should prepare a proposal that is ambitious enough to produce a useful outcome but limited enough to be completed between 5 October and 27 November 2026.

A well-structured project outline should address the following areas:

  1. Define the problem

Identify a specific challenge connected with blue carbon science, finance or policy. Avoid describing an issue that is too broad to address during an eight-week placement.

  1. Explain the project objective

State exactly what the project will investigate, develop, review or recommend.

  1. Describe the methodology

Explain whether the project will involve:

  • Literature review;
  • Data analysis;
  • Interviews;
  • Policy analysis;
  • Comparative research;
  • Financial analysis;
  • Laboratory work;
  • Fieldwork;
  • Stakeholder consultation; or
  • Preparation of a policy or technical output.
  1. Present achievable deliverables

Potential deliverables may include:

  • Research report;
  • Literature review;
  • Policy brief;
  • Technical guidance;
  • Financing framework;
  • Comparative case study;
  • Data product;
  • Practical recommendations; or
  • Regional knowledge document.
  1. Demonstrate regional value

Explain how the project could support decision-makers, researchers, communities or institutions in IORA Member States.

  1. Explain the collaborator’s contribution

Describe what knowledge, supervision, data access or technical support the nominated collaborator will provide.

  1. Address feasibility

Confirm that the project can be completed within the placement period and disclose any fieldwork, laboratory, equipment or sample-related requirements.

This approach aligns the proposal with the information requested in the application form and the official requirement that projects be focused, achievable and meaningful within the program period.

Eligible Countries

Applications are open to citizens or residents of the following 23 IORA Member States:

  1. Australia
  2. Bangladesh
  3. Comoros
  4. France
  5. India
  6. Indonesia
  7. Iran
  8. Kenya
  9. Madagascar
  10. Malaysia
  11. Maldives
  12. Mauritius
  13. Mozambique
  14. Oman
  15. Seychelles
  16. Singapore
  17. Somalia
  18. South Africa
  19. Sri Lanka
  20. Tanzania
  21. Thailand
  22. United Arab Emirates
  23. Yemen

How to Apply

Applications must be completed through the official Blue Carbon Hub online application form.

Applicants should follow these steps:

  1. Review the official program call brief.
  2. Read the complete Frequently Asked Questions document.
  3. Confirm citizenship or residency eligibility.
  4. Verify that the highest degree was completed within the permitted six-year period.
  5. Develop a focused project under science, finance or policy.
  6. Contact and confirm a proposed collaborator, where possible.
  7. Request a support letter from the host institution.
  8. Prepare and update the CV.
  9. Complete all questions in the online form.
  10. Upload the required documents.
  11. Review the application carefully.
  12. Submit it before midnight on 7 August 2026.

The official application portal is available through the Blue Carbon Hub’s current calls page. Click HERE, to apply.

Contact Information

For general questions about the Early Career Ocean Professionals Program, project eligibility or the application process, applicants should contact the Blue Carbon Hub through the email address published in the call documents.

Applicants seeking assistance in identifying a project collaborator or confirming whether a project idea is within scope may contact Mat Vanderklift with a short project summary before applying.

For questions related specifically to the IORA endorsement process, applicants should contact the IORA Secretariat representative identified in the official FAQ.

Conclusion

The Blue Carbon Hub Early Career Ocean Professionals Program 2026 offers emerging professionals an important opportunity to spend eight weeks in Perth, Australia, developing practical knowledge and solutions for the conservation and restoration of blue carbon ecosystems.

The program combines professional development, regional collaboration and project-based learning. Participants will have the opportunity to work on original research, reviews, policy briefs, financial analysis or other innovative activities addressing blue carbon challenges across the Indian Ocean region.

With major airfare, accommodation, eligible visa application expenses and per diem costs covered, the program substantially reduces the financial barriers associated with an international professional placement. Applicants should nevertheless understand that the program does not provide a salary, offers limited funding for field activities and generally requires participants to stay in shared accommodation.

Eligible candidates should begin preparing their project outlines, institutional support letters, CVs and collaborator information immediately. Complete applications must be submitted by midnight on 7 August 2026, and applicants are expected to receive the outcome by 31 August 2026

For more global opportunities, VISIT HERE.

The post Blue Carbon Hub Early Career Ocean Professionals Program 2026: Fully Funded Eight-Week Placement in Perth, Australia appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.

item.title: COP31 Türkiye 2026: Everything You Need to Know About the Global Climate Conference in Antalya
item.dc:creator: Phionah Tumusiime
item.description:
COP31 Türkiye 2026: A Global Climate Conference Focused on Turning Climate Commitments into Action The 31st United Nations Climate Change Conference, known as COP31, will take place in Türkiye in 2026, bringing together world leaders, governments, climate experts, scientists, businesses, young people, civil society organisations and other stakeholders to discuss one of the most important […]

The post COP31 Türkiye 2026: Everything You Need to Know About the Global Climate Conference in Antalya appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
COP31 Türkiye 2026: A Global Climate Conference Focused on Turning Climate Commitments into Action

The 31st United Nations Climate Change Conference, known as COP31, will take place in Türkiye in 2026, bringing together world leaders, governments, climate experts, scientists, businesses, young people, civil society organisations and other stakeholders to discuss one of the most important challenges facing humanity: climate change.

Hosted in the coastal city of Antalya, COP31 is expected to serve as a major platform for international climate negotiations and global cooperation. Under the central message of moving from dialogue and commitments to practical implementation, the conference aims to strengthen international cooperation and accelerate real-world climate action.

The conference will be held under the framework of the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change, commonly known as the UNFCCC. Since the adoption of the UNFCCC in 1992, the annual Conference of the Parties has become the highest-level international platform for countries to negotiate collective responses to climate change.

COP31 will mark the 31st session of this global climate process.

With climate change increasingly affecting communities, economies, food systems, cities, oceans and public health, the conference is expected to focus heavily on implementation, investment, innovation and the delivery of climate solutions.

What Is COP?

COP stands for the Conference of the Parties. It is the annual global climate summit held by countries that are parties to the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change.

The conference brings together representatives from nearly every country in the world to negotiate and advance international action on climate change.

Participants at COP may include:

  • Heads of state and government
  • Ministers and government officials
  • Climate negotiators
  • Scientists and researchers
  • Environmental experts
  • Businesses and investors
  • International organisations
  • Civil society organisations
  • Indigenous representatives
  • Youth leaders and climate activists
  • Academics and educators
  • Journalists and media organisations
  • Development partners
  • Climate finance institutions

COP provides a platform for countries to discuss issues such as:

  • Reducing greenhouse gas emissions
  • Limiting global temperature increases
  • Climate finance
  • Renewable energy
  • Adaptation to climate change
  • Loss and damage
  • Food security
  • Biodiversity
  • Climate-resilient infrastructure
  • Sustainable cities
  • Green industrialisation
  • Ocean protection
  • Technology and innovation
  • Youth participation in climate action

The process is particularly important because climate change is a global challenge that cannot be addressed by one country acting alone.

The History Behind COP31

COP31 is part of a long history of international climate cooperation.

1992: The United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change

The UNFCCC was adopted in 1992 and established the foundation for global climate negotiations.

Its purpose is to promote international cooperation to address climate change and reduce the risks associated with rising greenhouse gas emissions.

1997: The Kyoto Protocol

The Kyoto Protocol was adopted in 1997 and introduced legally binding emissions reduction targets for certain developed countries.

It represented one of the earliest major international attempts to create a structured global response to climate change.

2015: The Paris Agreement

The Paris Agreement, adopted in 2015, marked a major turning point in international climate diplomacy.

Countries agreed to work towards limiting the increase in global average temperatures and to strengthen their climate ambitions over time.

COP31 will continue this global climate process, with a strong emphasis on moving from commitments and negotiations to implementation and measurable action.

COP31 Türkiye 2026: A Turning Point for the Planet

Türkiye is positioning COP31 as a major opportunity to accelerate climate action at a time when the consequences of climate change are becoming increasingly visible around the world.

The conference is expected to focus on the need to transform climate commitments into concrete results.

This means moving beyond simply announcing climate targets and focusing on questions such as:

  • How will climate commitments be implemented?
  • How will climate projects be financed?
  • How can countries accelerate the transition to clean energy?
  • How can cities become more resilient?
  • How can industries reduce emissions while continuing to support economic development?
  • How can developing countries access the finance and technology they need?
  • How can young people become active participants in climate decision-making?
  • How can climate action support food security, health and sustainable development?

The central ambition is to help bridge the gap between climate pledges and practical delivery.

Türkiye’s Vision for COP31

Türkiye is presenting COP31 as a platform for dialogue, cooperation and implementation.

According to the COP31 vision, the world is facing a period of increasing fragmentation and uncertainty. In such an environment, climate action requires international trust, fairness and cooperation.

The conference is therefore expected to place significant emphasis on:

  1. Dialogue between countries and stakeholders
  2. International cooperation
  3. Climate finance
  4. Technology and innovation
  5. Implementation of existing commitments
  6. Inclusive participation
  7. Climate resilience
  8. Economic transformation
  9. Youth and education
  10. Practical solutions that can be scaled globally

Türkiye’s President, Recep Tayyip Erdoğan, has described COP31 as an opportunity to move from negotiation to implementation.

The overall vision is to encourage countries and stakeholders to strengthen cooperation and convert climate commitments into real action.

COP31 Action Agenda: Major Areas of Focus

The COP31 Action Agenda identifies several important areas where international climate action can be accelerated.

These focus areas cover the environment, energy, food, cities, industry, health, youth, oceans and climate finance.

1. Zero Waste

Waste management is an important part of the global climate challenge.

Poorly managed waste can contribute to greenhouse gas emissions, environmental pollution and the loss of valuable resources.

COP31 will highlight the potential of zero-waste approaches and circular economy solutions.

The focus includes:

  • Reducing waste generation
  • Reusing materials
  • Recycling
  • Improving waste management systems
  • Reducing methane emissions from waste
  • Promoting circular economy models
  • Creating more sustainable production and consumption systems

A circular economy aims to reduce the amount of waste generated by keeping materials and products in use for as long as possible.

This can help reduce pressure on natural resources while creating new opportunities for sustainable businesses and innovation.

2. Oceans and Seas

The world’s oceans and seas play a major role in the climate system.

They support marine biodiversity, provide livelihoods for millions of people and help absorb carbon dioxide from the atmosphere.

However, oceans are increasingly threatened by:

  • Rising temperatures
  • Ocean acidification
  • Plastic pollution
  • Overfishing
  • Coastal degradation
  • Rising sea levels
  • Loss of marine biodiversity

COP31 will highlight the importance of protecting marine ecosystems and developing solutions based on blue carbon.

Blue carbon refers to carbon captured and stored by marine and coastal ecosystems such as mangroves, seagrass meadows and salt marshes.

Protecting these ecosystems can help strengthen climate resilience while supporting biodiversity and coastal communities.

3. Food Security

Climate change is increasingly affecting agriculture and food systems.

Extreme weather events, droughts, floods, rising temperatures and changing rainfall patterns can affect food production and increase food insecurity.

COP31 will focus on advancing sustainable and resilient food systems.

This includes the need to:

  • Improve agricultural resilience
  • Support sustainable farming
  • Protect food production from climate risks
  • Reduce emissions from food systems
  • Improve access to food
  • Support farmers and rural communities
  • Promote climate-smart agriculture
  • Reduce food waste

Building climate-resilient food systems is essential to protecting communities and ensuring that populations can access nutritious and affordable food in the future.

4. Climate-Resilient Cities

Cities are at the centre of the climate challenge.

Urban areas are responsible for a significant share of global emissions, but they are also highly vulnerable to climate-related risks.

Climate-resilient cities are designed to withstand and adapt to challenges such as:

  • Flooding
  • Extreme heat
  • Water shortages
  • Rising sea levels
  • Storms
  • Air pollution
  • Infrastructure damage

COP31 will promote solutions for creating cities that are more sustainable, resilient and liveable.

This may include:

  • Climate-resilient buildings
  • Sustainable urban planning
  • Public transportation
  • Energy-efficient infrastructure
  • Green spaces
  • Better water management
  • Renewable energy
  • Sustainable construction

The objective is to ensure that cities remain safe and liveable as climate pressures increase.

5. Climate Implementation Bridge

One of the central concepts of COP31 is the Climate Implementation Bridge, also known as CIB.

The Climate Implementation Bridge is designed to help connect climate commitments with investment, projects and actual delivery.

Many countries have already announced climate targets and policies. However, implementation often faces challenges such as:

  • Lack of financing
  • Limited technical capacity
  • Insufficient technology
  • Weak infrastructure
  • Gaps between public and private sectors
  • Difficulties converting plans into projects

The Climate Implementation Bridge seeks to help address these challenges by connecting priorities with investment and delivery.

The broader objective is to move from:

Commitments → Investment → Implementation → Results

This approach reflects the growing global demand for practical climate action.

6. Youth and Education

Young people are among the most important stakeholders in the global climate movement.

They will live with the long-term consequences of decisions made today.

COP31 aims to position young people not only as individuals inheriting climate risks but also as architects of climate solutions.

Youth participation may involve:

  • Climate advocacy
  • Innovation
  • Education
  • Research
  • Entrepreneurship
  • Policy engagement
  • Environmental campaigns
  • Community projects

Education is also critical to building climate awareness and preparing future generations to address climate challenges.

The conference’s focus on youth and education reflects the importance of ensuring that young people have access to knowledge, opportunities and platforms to participate in climate action.

7. Green Industrialisation

Industries around the world are under increasing pressure to reduce their environmental impact.

At the same time, industrial development remains important for economic growth, employment and national development.

COP31 will focus on green industrialisation as a way of supporting economic development while reducing carbon emissions.

Key areas may include:

  • Clean technologies
  • Renewable energy
  • Energy efficiency
  • Low-carbon manufacturing
  • Sustainable production
  • Green jobs
  • Innovation
  • Industrial decarbonisation

The goal is to develop low-carbon industrial pathways that support economic competitiveness while contributing to a just transition.

8. Clean Energy Transition

The transition to clean energy is one of the most important issues in the global climate response.

COP31 will focus on the need to accelerate access to clean, secure and affordable energy.

The clean energy transition includes:

  • Renewable energy
  • Solar power
  • Wind energy
  • Energy efficiency
  • Modern electricity systems
  • Energy storage
  • Clean technologies
  • Reduced dependence on high-emission energy sources

However, the transition must also ensure that energy remains reliable and affordable.

For many developing countries, increasing access to electricity remains a major development priority.

Therefore, the global energy transition must consider both climate objectives and the need for economic development and energy access.

9. Rio Synergy

Climate change is closely connected to biodiversity loss and desertification.

These challenges are often treated separately, but they are deeply interconnected.

The Rio Synergy approach seeks to promote integrated solutions that address:

  • Climate change
  • Biodiversity loss
  • Land degradation
  • Desertification

For example, restoring degraded land can help:

  • Capture carbon
  • Protect biodiversity
  • Improve agricultural productivity
  • Support local communities
  • Reduce desertification

Integrated environmental solutions can therefore deliver multiple benefits at the same time.

10. Dynamic and Resilient Health Systems

Climate change has serious consequences for public health.

Extreme heat, air pollution, infectious diseases, food insecurity, water shortages and climate-related disasters can all affect human health.

COP31 will focus on the need to build health systems that are:

  • Climate-resilient
  • Low-carbon
  • Equitable
  • Prepared for climate-related emergencies

Climate-resilient health systems should be able to continue providing essential healthcare services even during extreme weather events and other climate-related crises.

The focus on health also highlights the fact that climate change is not only an environmental issue. It is also a major social, economic and public health challenge.

COP31 Venues and Participation Areas

COP31 will include different participation areas designed to serve different purposes.

The Blue Zone

The Blue Zone is the official negotiation area managed under the authority of the United Nations.

It is expected to serve as the main area for:

  • Official negotiations
  • Government delegations
  • UN activities
  • Diplomatic discussions
  • Formal meetings
  • Accredited participants

The Blue Zone is generally associated with the formal intergovernmental process of COP.

The Green Zone

The Green Zone is organised under the coordination of the host country.

It is designed to be more inclusive and participatory.

The Green Zone may provide opportunities for:

  • Public engagement
  • Businesses
  • Civil society
  • Youth
  • Researchers
  • Innovators
  • Environmental organisations
  • Educational institutions
  • Climate solution providers

The Green Zone is expected to serve as a space where a wider range of stakeholders can participate in climate discussions and showcase solutions.

COP31 Host City: Antalya, Türkiye

COP31 will be hosted in Antalya, one of Türkiye’s internationally recognised cities.

Antalya is known for its coastal location and connection to the Mediterranean Sea.

The city provides an important setting for discussions about:

  • Oceans and marine ecosystems
  • Coastal resilience
  • Tourism and climate change
  • Sustainable cities
  • Climate adaptation
  • Environmental protection

The COP31 vision presents Antalya as a meeting point for global climate dialogue.

The city is expected to welcome participants from around the world for the major international climate event.

Antalya Airport

Antalya Airport will serve as an important access point for international and domestic visitors attending the conference.

Participants travelling to COP31 will be able to access information about the airport and transportation arrangements through the official COP31 visitor information channels.

Antalya COP31 Venue

The official COP31 venue will host activities connected to the conference.

Participants will be able to access information about:

  • The conference areas
  • Venue arrangements
  • Participation procedures
  • Visitor information
  • Event locations
  • Official programme details

Visitors are encouraged to consult official COP31 information before making travel or participation arrangements.

COP31 Volunteer Programme

One of the opportunities connected to COP31 is the Volunteer Programme.

The programme is designed for individuals interested in supporting climate action while gaining experience at a major international event.

The volunteer programme presents an opportunity to:

  • Support a major global climate conference
  • Gain experience in an international event environment
  • Engage with people from different countries and backgrounds
  • Learn more about climate action
  • Contribute to sustainability-related activities
  • Build professional and international networks

The official COP31 platform describes the programme as an opportunity to volunteer for climate action and leave a mark on the future.

Individuals interested in volunteering should review the official requirements and application instructions before submitting an application.

COP31 Academic Engagement and COP-SCIENCE Platform

COP31 is also opening opportunities for members of the academic community.

The COP-SCIENCE platform has been established to strengthen the contribution of academics and researchers to climate action.

Through the platform, academic participants may submit proposals related to:

  • Academic events
  • Research
  • Climate projects
  • Collaborations

This initiative is important because scientific research plays a critical role in shaping climate policies and solutions.

Researchers and academic institutions can contribute by:

  • Sharing evidence-based findings
  • Presenting innovative solutions
  • Developing partnerships
  • Supporting climate policy
  • Engaging with policymakers
  • Building international research collaborations

The COP-SCIENCE platform creates an opportunity for the academic community to contribute directly to the wider COP31 climate process.

COP31 Partnerships and Türkiye Pavilion Side Events

The COP31 Presidency has also opened applications for partnerships and Türkiye Pavilion Side Events.

National and international stakeholders may apply through the official application portals.

Eligible organisations and institutions may include stakeholders working in areas such as:

  • Climate action
  • Sustainable development
  • Renewable energy
  • Environmental protection
  • Climate finance
  • Research
  • Technology
  • Green industry
  • Youth engagement
  • Food security
  • Oceans
  • Health
  • Sustainable cities

Deadline

Applications for Partnerships and Türkiye Pavilion Side Events are open until 14 August 2026.

Applicants should carefully review the official requirements, application procedures and submission guidelines before applying.

COP31 and Bloomberg Philanthropies Partnership

The COP31 Presidency has also announced a partnership with Bloomberg Philanthropies to advance climate action ahead of the conference.

The partnership is intended to support the COP31 Presidency’s Action Agenda.

Its focus includes:

  • Mobilising climate finance
  • Bringing together business leaders
  • Engaging financial institutions
  • Accelerating implementation
  • Supporting climate action initiatives

The involvement of philanthropic organisations, businesses and financial institutions highlights the growing importance of partnerships in addressing climate change.

Governments alone cannot solve the climate crisis.

Climate action requires collaboration between:

  • Governments
  • Businesses
  • Investors
  • Philanthropic organisations
  • Researchers
  • Civil society
  • Local communities
  • Young people

The COP31 Mascot: Caretta

COP31 Antalya has introduced Caretta as its sea turtle mascot.

The mascot is inspired by the Caretta caretta, or loggerhead sea turtle, which is closely associated with the Mediterranean region.

The mascot represents:

  • The importance of oceans
  • Marine biodiversity
  • Coastal ecosystems
  • Climate awareness
  • Environmental protection

Caretta is presented as an ambassador for climate action and the story of the seas.

The mascot’s interactive experience takes visitors underwater to explore the importance of marine ecosystems and ocean protection.

The initiative also reflects COP31’s focus on making climate information more accessible and engaging for people of different ages and backgrounds.

Who Can Participate in COP31 Türkiye 2026?

COP31 is an international event involving participants from around the world.

Participation opportunities may be available to different groups depending on the specific programme.

Potential participants include:

Governments and Policymakers

Government representatives and national delegations play a central role in the official climate negotiations.

Scientists and Researchers

Researchers can contribute scientific evidence, research findings and climate solutions.

Universities and Academic Institutions

Universities and academic institutions may participate through research, events, collaborations and the COP-SCIENCE platform.

Businesses and Investors

Private-sector organisations can participate in climate partnerships, sustainable business initiatives and investment discussions.

Civil Society Organisations

Environmental and development organisations can contribute to discussions, advocacy and climate initiatives.

Youth

Young people can participate through youth programmes, climate advocacy, education, volunteering and other engagement opportunities.

Climate Activists

Climate activists and environmental advocates may participate through relevant programmes and accredited activities.

Educators

Teachers, trainers and educational institutions can contribute to climate awareness and education initiatives.

International Organisations

International organisations and development partners may participate in climate cooperation and implementation efforts.

Volunteers

Individuals interested in supporting the conference and gaining experience in an international event environment may apply to the Volunteer Programme when eligible.

Media Professionals

Journalists and media organisations may participate through relevant accreditation and media arrangements.

Participation requirements may differ significantly depending on whether an individual is applying as a volunteer, researcher, event organiser, official delegate, media professional or another category of participant.

Is COP31 Open to People from Africa?

COP31 is a global international climate conference.

Climate change affects every region of the world, including Africa.

African governments, organisations, researchers, young people, businesses, civil society groups and climate professionals may participate through relevant official channels and programmes.

Potential areas of interest for African participants may include:

  • Climate finance
  • Renewable energy
  • Food security
  • Climate adaptation
  • Sustainable cities
  • Agriculture
  • Green industrialisation
  • Youth climate action
  • Research
  • Climate technology
  • Environmental protection

Participation is not limited to one continent or nationality.

However, specific requirements depend on the particular COP31 opportunity being pursued.

Why COP31 Matters for Young People

Young people are increasingly becoming central to global climate action.

Climate change will have long-term consequences for today’s youth and future generations.

COP31’s focus on youth and education creates opportunities for young people to:

  • Learn about global climate policy
  • Participate in climate discussions
  • Connect with international stakeholders
  • Develop professional networks
  • Explore careers in sustainability
  • Participate in climate innovation
  • Engage in environmental advocacy
  • Gain international experience

Young people interested in climate change, international development, environmental policy, renewable energy, sustainability, research and related fields may find COP31 particularly relevant.

Why COP31 Matters for Students and Researchers

Students and researchers can benefit from the academic and scientific engagement opportunities connected to COP31.

The COP-SCIENCE platform may provide opportunities to submit proposals for academic events, research projects and collaborations.

This could be particularly relevant for individuals working in fields such as:

  • Climate science
  • Environmental studies
  • Political science
  • International relations
  • Economics
  • Energy
  • Agriculture
  • Public health
  • Engineering
  • Urban planning
  • Technology
  • Sustainable development

Academic participation can also help strengthen connections between research and policymaking.

Why COP31 Matters for Professionals and Organisations

COP31 is expected to attract a wide range of professionals and organisations.

For professionals, participation may provide opportunities to:

  • Expand international networks
  • Meet climate experts
  • Explore partnerships
  • Learn about global climate policy
  • Discover career opportunities
  • Engage with investors
  • Present ideas and solutions
  • Build international collaborations

For organisations, COP31 may provide a platform to:

  • Showcase climate solutions
  • Build partnerships
  • Participate in side events
  • Engage with policymakers
  • Connect with investors
  • Promote sustainability initiatives

Key COP31 Themes at a Glance

The major areas of focus include:

  1. Zero Waste
  2. Oceans and Seas
  3. Food Security
  4. Climate-Resilient Cities
  5. Climate Implementation Bridge
  6. Youth and Education
  7. Green Industrialisation
  8. Clean Energy Transition
  9. Rio Synergy
  10. Dynamic and Resilient Health Systems

Together, these areas reflect the broad nature of the climate crisis.

Climate change affects the environment, economy, health, food systems, energy, cities and future generations.

COP31 therefore aims to promote solutions that connect different sectors rather than addressing climate issues in isolation.

Important COP31 Deadline

Individuals and organisations interested in COP31-related opportunities should pay close attention to official deadlines.

The deadline specifically stated is:

Partnerships and Türkiye Pavilion Side Events Application Deadline: 14 August 2026

Other programmes, including volunteering and academic submissions, may have separate deadlines or application timelines.

Applicants should always confirm the latest requirements and deadlines through the relevant official COP31 application platform.

How to Prepare for COP31 Opportunities

Individuals interested in participating in COP31 should consider taking the following steps:

1. Identify the Relevant Opportunity

Determine whether you are interested in:

  • Volunteering
  • Academic engagement
  • Partnerships
  • Side events
  • Climate research
  • Youth participation
  • Media participation
  • Official conference attendance

2. Review the Eligibility Requirements

Different programmes may have different requirements.

Carefully check:

  • Age requirements
  • Professional requirements
  • Academic requirements
  • Nationality or residency requirements, where applicable
  • Organisational requirements
  • Application documents

3. Prepare Your Documents

Depending on the opportunity, applicants may need:

  • Curriculum vitae
  • Academic proposal
  • Project proposal
  • Organisation profile
  • Partnership concept
  • Personal statement
  • Identification documents
  • References

4. Submit Before the Deadline

Applications should be submitted before the official deadline.

For Partnerships and Türkiye Pavilion Side Events, the deadline is 14 August 2026.

 APPLY HERE

5. Follow Official Updates

COP31 is a major international event, and details may continue to develop as the conference approaches.

Interested participants should follow official updates relating to:

  • Applications
  • Accreditation
  • Programmes
  • Travel
  • Venue arrangements
  • Events
  • Participation requirements

For more information, visit COP31 Website HERE

Why COP31 Could Be a Major Opportunity for Climate-Focused Individuals

COP31 offers more than a traditional conference experience.

It represents a major international platform where global climate priorities, policies, investments and solutions come together.

For students and young professionals, participation could provide exposure to:

  • International climate diplomacy
  • Global policymaking
  • Sustainability
  • Environmental science
  • Renewable energy
  • Climate finance
  • International development
  • Green entrepreneurship

For organisations, it could create opportunities for:

  • Partnerships
  • Networking
  • Visibility
  • Investment
  • Collaboration
  • Knowledge sharing

For governments, COP31 will provide a platform to negotiate and advance international climate action.

For researchers, it offers opportunities to share knowledge and influence policy.

For young people, it provides a platform to participate in shaping the future.

For more global volunteer opportunities visit OFY Website HERE

Conclusion

COP31 Türkiye 2026 is set to bring the global climate community together in Antalya for the 31st session of the Conference of the Parties under the United Nations climate process.

With the central focus on moving from dialogue and consensus to action and implementation, the conference aims to address some of the most urgent challenges facing the planet.

Its broad Action Agenda covers critical issues including zero waste, oceans and seas, food security, climate-resilient cities, youth and education, green industrialisation, clean energy, climate finance, biodiversity, desertification and climate-resilient health systems.

The conference will also create opportunities for researchers, academics, young people, volunteers, businesses, civil society organisations and other stakeholders to contribute to global climate action.

Among the opportunities already highlighted are the COP31 Volunteer Programme, the COP-SCIENCE academic engagement platform, and applications for Partnerships and Türkiye Pavilion Side Events.

The deadline for Partnerships and Türkiye Pavilion Side Events applications is 14 August 2026.

As climate change continues to affect communities and economies across the world, COP31 represents an important opportunity to strengthen international cooperation, promote practical solutions and accelerate the transition from climate commitments to real implementation.

For individuals and organisations interested in climate action, sustainability, environmental protection, research, youth engagement, clean energy and international development, COP31 Türkiye 2026 could provide a valuable platform for learning, networking, collaboration and global impact.

The post COP31 Türkiye 2026: Everything You Need to Know About the Global Climate Conference in Antalya appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.

item.title: GAEC Africa Archivist-in-Residence 2026 in Senegal: Apply to Build a Decolonial Digital Archive in Saint-Louis
item.dc:creator: Muhammad Chishti
item.description:
About the GAEC Africa Archivist-in-Residence Opportunity The GAEC Africa Archivist-in-Residence programme has been established to document, organize, interpret, and digitally preserve materials connected to Groundings, a decolonial convening held in 2025. According to the opportunity listing, the selected Archivist-in-Residence will: Spend one month residing in Saint-Louis, Senegal; Work from GAEC-Africa’s community space; Develop a digital […]

The post GAEC Africa Archivist-in-Residence 2026 in Senegal: Apply to Build a Decolonial Digital Archive in Saint-Louis appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
About the GAEC Africa Archivist-in-Residence Opportunity

The GAEC Africa Archivist-in-Residence programme has been established to document, organize, interpret, and digitally preserve materials connected to Groundings, a decolonial convening held in 2025.

According to the opportunity listing, the selected Archivist-in-Residence will:

  • Spend one month residing in Saint-Louis, Senegal;
  • Work from GAEC-Africa’s community space;
  • Develop a digital archive of the 2025 Groundings convening;
  • Produce an archive that is original, engaging, and rich in source material; and
  • Engage meaningfully with GAEC-Africa’s student-activist community.

This is described as GAEC-Africa’s inaugural Archivist-in-Residence opportunity, meaning that the selected applicant will help shape not only the archive itself but potentially the working approach for future archival residencies and documentation initiatives.

What Is Groundings?

Groundings is identified in the call as GAEC-Africa’s 2025 decolonial convening. Although the detailed programme of the 2025 gathering is not included in the publicly accessible Rhizome summary, the wider intellectual context of GAEC-Africa’s Groundings work is rooted in decolonial thought, Black radical traditions, political economy, community learning, and the relationship between locally situated knowledge and global structures.

GAEC-Africa’s seminars and community projects have previously created spaces in which students, scholars, activists, and community members examine issues including postcolonial societies, capitalism, resistance, gender, labour, imperialism, and global Blackness. These activities seek to connect critical theory with the social, cultural, and political experiences of communities in and around Saint-Louis.

The proposed digital archive will therefore be more than a conventional collection of files. It is expected to preserve the intellectual exchanges, community participation, creative practices, discussions, and forms of knowledge production that emerged from the convening.

About GAEC-Africa

The Group of Action and Critical Study-Africa, commonly known as GAEC-Africa, is an intellectual, artistic, and community-based ecosystem founded in 2020. It brings together researchers, artists, students, activists, professionals, volunteers, community members, and critical thinkers to co-produce knowledge from Africa and the Global South.

GAEC-Africa describes itself through four interconnected identities:

  1. An association of academics;
  2. A critical study group;
  3. An African initiative; and
  4. A decolonial collective.

Its work is concerned with historical, cultural, political, epistemic, economic, racial, and social injustices. It promotes research combined with action through seminars, community workshops, advocacy, public awareness initiatives, experiential learning, curriculum development, cultural programming, and community engagement.

GAEC-Africa’s Three Main Areas of Work

GAEC-Africa organizes its activities around three principal ecosystems.

1. Centre Universitaire Panafricain et Vernaculaire

This platform concentrates on research, popular and critical knowledge, counter-archives, seminars, publications, podcasts, fellowships, and experimental approaches to knowledge production.

Its programmes seek to provide learning opportunities grounded in critical theory, Pan-Africanism, decolonial thought, and the Black radical tradition. The seminars are intended to connect academic inquiry with community experiences and create spaces where knowledge can be produced collectively rather than transferred only through conventional institutions.

2. Ndar Demb ak Tey

Ndar Demb ak Tey focuses on residencies, cultural production, exhibitions, performances, curatorial initiatives, festivals, and community events.

Its research and archiving mission seeks to reinterpret the history of Saint-Louis through oral histories, objects, memories, struggles, experiences, and accounts contributed by the city’s inhabitants. GAEC-Africa describes this work as both a dynamic oral-history museum project and a pedagogical programme concerned with the contemporary experiences of societies in the Global South.

The Archivist-in-Residence opportunity is strongly aligned with this institutional commitment to community-based archiving and the preservation of situated knowledge.

3. Black Teraanga Bloc

The Black Teraanga Bloc supports public interventions, citizen initiatives, campaigns, community mobilization, dialogues, and actions focused on hospitality, solidarity, social justice, and public engagement.

Together, these three areas connect research, art, archiving, education, activism, cultural practice, and community participation.

What Will the Archivist-in-Residence Do?

The central responsibility will be to transform the documentation of the Groundings convening into a coherent and accessible digital archive.

The listing does not provide a detailed technical terms of reference in its publicly accessible summary. However, producing the type of digital archive described in the call would ordinarily involve several interconnected stages.

1. Review and Inventory Available Materials

The resident may need to review the materials produced before, during, and after Groundings. Depending on what GAEC-Africa has collected, these could include:

  • Photographs;
  • Video recordings;
  • Audio recordings;
  • Event programmes;
  • Presentation materials;
  • Participant notes;
  • Transcripts;
  • Posters and visual designs;
  • Interviews;
  • Written reflections;
  • Biographical information;
  • Reading lists;
  • Workshop outputs;
  • Correspondence;
  • Community testimonies; and
  • Other documentary or artistic materials.

These examples illustrate the types of content that may form part of a convening archive; they should not be treated as a confirmed inventory of the materials available.

2. Develop an Archival Structure

The Archivist-in-Residence may be expected to determine how the material should be classified, described, connected, and presented.

This could involve developing:

  • A clear archival hierarchy;
  • Categories and subcategories;
  • A file-naming system;
  • Descriptive metadata;
  • Contributor information;
  • Chronological or thematic groupings;
  • Searchable keywords;
  • Contextual descriptions;
  • Cross-references between related materials; and
  • Guidelines for the future addition of content.

A strong digital archive should enable users to understand not only what an item is but also who produced it, why it matters, how it relates to the convening, and what historical or intellectual context surrounds it.

3. Digitally Curate the Groundings Story

The call asks for a digital archive that is dynamic, original, and material-packed. This wording suggests that GAEC-Africa is seeking more than a basic online storage folder or document repository.

The resident may need to consider how different forms of material can be combined to communicate the history and significance of Groundings. This could include thematic collections, multimedia narratives, participant profiles, timelines, annotated galleries, interactive pathways, or other approaches to digital storytelling.

The final structure should preserve the complexity of the convening without removing the voices, context, and relationships that gave the materials their meaning.

4. Engage the Student-Activist Community

Community engagement is an expressly stated component of the residency. The successful applicant will work closely with GAEC-Africa’s local student-activist community rather than developing the archive in isolation.

This engagement may help the resident:

  • Understand the history and purpose of the convening;
  • Identify important themes and moments;
  • Obtain context for specific materials;
  • Clarify names, dates, locations, and events;
  • Understand how participants want their contributions represented;
  • Collect additional reflections or descriptions;
  • Build local archival and digital-documentation capacity; and
  • Ensure that the archive reflects community perspectives.

A participatory process is especially important for a decolonial archive. Decisions regarding classification, description, authorship, access, and interpretation can determine whose knowledge is made visible and whose voice is marginalized.

Why This Digital Archive Matters

Archives are not neutral collections. The decisions made about what is preserved, how it is categorized, who is credited, and how material is accessed influence how future users understand an event, institution, or community.

The GAEC Africa Archivist-in-Residence project has the potential to contribute in several important ways.

Preservation of Community Knowledge

Events such as Groundings may generate valuable discussions, creative materials, recordings, and collective reflections. Without deliberate preservation, these materials can become fragmented, inaccessible, or lost.

A structured digital archive can protect this knowledge and make it available for future research, education, artistic practice, community organizing, and institutional learning.

Development of a Counter-Archive

GAEC-Africa’s institutional work includes the production of critical knowledge, popular knowledge, and counter-archives. A counter-archive can challenge dominant historical narratives by preserving materials, perspectives, and lived experiences that have often been excluded from conventional institutions.

The Groundings archive may therefore help document intellectual and political work from Saint-Louis on its own terms rather than filtering it exclusively through external academic or cultural frameworks.

Connection Between Local and Global Conversations

GAEC-Africa’s work is locally situated in Senegal while remaining connected to African, diasporic, and Global South intellectual networks.

A well-developed online archive could allow students, researchers, artists, activists, and community organizations outside Saint-Louis to engage with the ideas and materials generated through Groundings.

Institutional Learning

The residency can also help GAEC-Africa strengthen its internal systems for documentation, digital preservation, metadata management, consent, access, and long-term archival maintenance.

Because this is the inaugural Archivist-in-Residence programme, the resident’s methods and recommendations could potentially inform how the organization manages future events and collections.

Who Is the Opportunity Best Suited For?

The publicly accessible summary does not prescribe a particular academic degree, professional certification, age range, or minimum number of years of experience.

Based on the nature of the assignment, it appears particularly relevant to:

  • Archivists and digital archivists;
  • Librarians and information-management professionals;
  • Digital-humanities researchers;
  • Public historians;
  • Curators and cultural heritage professionals;
  • Documentary artists;
  • Multimedia storytellers;
  • Digital artists and creative technologists;
  • Researchers working on African, Black, or decolonial studies;
  • Oral-history practitioners;
  • Museum and collection professionals;
  • Web-based exhibition designers;
  • Community archive practitioners; and
  • Individuals experienced in participatory documentation.

Applicants do not necessarily need to fit only one professional category. The residency appears interdisciplinary and could suit someone who combines archival practice with research, artistic production, digital design, documentary work, or community engagement.

Relevant Skills for the Residency

A competitive applicant would likely be able to demonstrate several of the following capabilities:

Archival and Information-Management Skills

  • Organizing physical and digital records;
  • Designing archive structures;
  • Cataloguing and classification;
  • Creating metadata;
  • Developing naming conventions and taxonomies;
  • Managing multiple file formats;
  • Applying digital-preservation practices; and
  • Documenting provenance, ownership, and context.

Digital and Multimedia Skills

  • Developing digital exhibitions or online archives;
  • Working with content-management systems;
  • Editing photographs, audio, or video;
  • Digitizing documents and media;
  • Managing large digital collections;
  • Designing accessible user experiences;
  • Building interactive timelines or multimedia narratives; and
  • Preparing web-ready archival content.

Research and Writing Skills

  • Conducting contextual and historical research;
  • Writing accurate descriptions;
  • Summarizing complex discussions;
  • Interviewing participants;
  • Fact-checking names, dates, and events;
  • Developing interpretive texts; and
  • Presenting academic or community knowledge in an accessible form.

Community-Engagement Skills

  • Working respectfully with students, activists, artists, and local communities;
  • Facilitating collaborative decisions;
  • Handling sensitive or personal information ethically;
  • Obtaining and documenting consent;
  • Recognizing community ownership of knowledge;
  • Listening to different perspectives; and
  • Avoiding extractive approaches to documentation.

Understanding of Decolonial Archival Practice

Because the archive concerns a decolonial convening and will be developed within an organization explicitly committed to decolonial knowledge production, applicants should understand that archival categories and institutional practices can reproduce unequal power relationships.

Experience or demonstrated interest in African studies, Black studies, colonial history, oral history, community archives, epistemic justice, or related areas would therefore be valuable.

Residency Location and Duration
